behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: The extractor is an LLM-based reviewer. Real failures occur when the LLM doesn't correctly identify certain key term types, particularly in contracts with non-standard phrasing. Mocking the extractor would always pass if configured to return 'complete' results, hiding failures where the real LLM misses subtle or non-obvious terms. The failure boundary is crossed when switching from mock to real LLM behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real clause extractor on a diverse set of test contracts (e.g., simple service agreement, complex software license, real-world example with elliptical language) and verify that all expected key term categories (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) are identified in the extraction output. Include contracts where key terms are expressed indirectly or in unusual formats to catch real LLM limitations.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: Detecting indirect or euphemistic risk language requires the nuanced reasoning of a real LLM. The behavior explicitly centers on handling subtle phrasing, not just exact matches to a policy. Mocking the assessor with test-case-specific risk flags would hide failures where the real LLM misses euphemistic variants, synonyms, or context-dependent risks. Testing with mocks would always return the 'correct' flags and wouldn't catch real LLM limitations in subtle detection.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real risk assessor with test contracts containing problematic language in multiple forms: direct phrasing (e.g., 'unlimited liability'), euphemistic variants (e.g., 'flexible responsibility terms'), and indirect references (e.g., 'parties may interpret obligations as needed'). Verify that the real assessor flags all forms as risky according to the configured risk policy, capturing LLM reasoning about context and implication.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: Determining compliance with jurisdiction-specific regulations is complex knowledge work that either requires a real LLM reasoning about regulations or real calls to an external regulatory database API. Mocking compliance results would hide failures where the checker misinterprets requirements, misses applicable regulations for the jurisdiction, or applies outdated/incorrect rules. The real failure boundary depends on whether the checker uses LLM reasoning (Agentic) or external API calls (System)—given the context of AI reviewers, LLM-based is most likely.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real compliance checker on test contracts for multiple jurisdictions (e.g., US, EU with GDPR implications, UK post-Brexit scenarios) where regulatory requirements are known and vary by region. For each jurisdiction, verify that the checker correctly identifies required/missing compliance measures. Include edge cases where regulations conflict or overlap to catch real LLM reasoning gaps.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: The merge step is deterministic logic that combines structured reviewer outputs. Real failures are in the merge implementation: dropping assessments, losing attribution, or malforming the output structure. These can be caught by unit tests using mocked reviewer outputs with well-defined formats. The merge logic itself doesn't depend on real LLM behavior; it depends on correctly handling data structures. Mocking reviewer outputs is sufficient if the input schema is known.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the merge function by providing mock outputs from the three reviewers, including cases where two or more reviewers assess the same clause differently (e.g., extractor: 'standard', risk assessor: 'high risk'). Verify that the merged output preserves both assessments with clear attribution (e.g., 'extractor: standard | risk assessor: high risk'). Test edge cases: same clause flagged by all three with conflicting levels, timestamp conflicts, overlapping clause boundaries.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: The full pipeline orchestrates three real LLM-based reviewers in parallel plus a merge step. Real failures include: timeouts when any LLM is slow, race conditions in collecting parallel results, format mismatches between real reviewer outputs and merge expectations, or exceptions in dispatch/collection logic that lose a reviewer's findings. Testing with mocked reviewers would hide LLM latency issues, real output variability, and true parallelization bugs. This requires multiple agentic steps coordinated together.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline end-to-end with a real contract (not mocked reviewers). Verify that: all three reviewers complete and return findings, the merge step receives all three outputs, the final unified report contains all findings from all three reviewers (no data loss), and the pipeline completes within expected latency bounds. Include contracts that are large (stress test LLM timeouts), ambiguous (to trigger varied reviewer interpretations), and have real-world complexity to catch integration failures.
